results.possibleIdentity: Judy (Judith) Lawson was a key administrative figure and editor for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded in 1966 by Billy Klüver, Robert Rauschenberg, Fred Waldhauer, and Robert Whitman to promote collaborations between artists and engineers. Lawson served as the editor of the organization's influential newsletter and journal, Techne, which documented the E.A.T. projects and philosophical goals during the late 1960s (notably in 1969 and 1970). Based in New York City, she played a significant role in the E.A.T. Service Bureau, an initiative that matched artists with technical expertise for projects like the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka. In later years, she has been identified as a freelance writer and researcher with interests in design and art history.

Judy Lawson is a contemporary artist and jewelry maker based in Kansas. A native Kansan, she spent 26 years as an educator before transitioning into a career in art following her retirement. Her work primarily focuses on metalwork and jewelry making, characterized by an earthy style and the use of natural patterns, textures, colorful stones, and rich metals. She is a teaching artist at Mark Arts (formerly the Wichita Center for the Arts) and her work has been exhibited at various galleries, including the Steckline Gallery and Wichita City Arts.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Stokes was an engineer and technical collaborator associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. Working as an engineer at Bell Laboratories, he was a key figure in some of the most significant projects organized by E.A.T., which sought to foster collaborations between the arts and sciences. In 1966, Stokes served as one of the primary engineers for artist Robert Whitman's performance piece, 'Two Holes of Water - 3,' presented during the '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' event at the 69th Regiment Armory in New York City. His most prominent contribution to the art-tech movement was his work on the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. For this project, Stokes was responsible for the technical realization and engineering of the pavilion's centerpiece: a massive 90-foot-diameter spherical mirror made of aluminized Mylar. This mirror, designed in collaboration with Robert Whitman and other engineers like John Pearce, allowed visitors to see three-dimensional real images floating in space. Stokes is often recognized in historical accounts of the period as a bridge between high-level physics/engineering and experimental contemporary art.

Robert Stokes (1908–1980) was an American animator and artist who was active in the early animation industry and whose legacy continued through re-releases of his work in the late 1960s and 1970s. He is best known for his work at Walt Disney Productions during the 1930s and 1940s, where he contributed to several landmark feature films. He served as an animator for 'Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs' (1937), specifically credited with helping to develop and draw the Evil Queen. He also worked on the 'Nutcracker Suite' segment of 'Fantasia' (1940) and on 'Pinocchio' (1940). While primarily known for these early roles, his work remained highly influential during the psychedelic revival of 'Fantasia' in the late 1960s and early 1970s when the film was reissued as a 'trip film.' He passed away in California in 1980 at the age of 71.

results.possibleIdentity: Shari Dienes (historically and commonly known as Sari Dienes, 1898–1992) was a sculptor and intermedia artist active in the New York avant-garde scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. She lived in the artistic community of Stony Point, New York, where she was a close friend and neighbor of John Cage. She was associated with the USCO (Us Company) collective in Garnersville and worked closely with pioneers of video and performance art such as Nam June Paik and Jud Yalkut. Her work often bridged art and technology; notably, in 1969, she created giant political effigies equipped with loudspeakers for heads, which were featured in intermedia performances and films. She is documented as a participant in films and collaborative projects alongside John Cage, Shigeko Kubota, and Nam June Paik, and was part of the social and professional circle that founded and promoted the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

Shari Lewis (born Phyllis Naomi Hurwitz, 1933–1998) was an American ventriloquist, puppeteer, and multi-disciplinary artist active throughout the 1960s and 1970s. Based in New York and later Los Angeles, she was a major television figure who incorporated artistic collaboration into her performances. In the mid-1960s, she was part of the vibrant New York creative scene where experimental artists such as Ralph Lee (who later founded the Village Halloween Parade) crafted props and masks for her shows. While primarily known for children's entertainment and her character Lamb Chop, she was a native New Yorker active during the founding era of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and occasionally appeared in contexts alongside avant-garde performers in the city's off-off-Broadway circuit.

results.possibleIdentity: Nicholas Michael Roukes (1922–2014) was a Canadian-American artist, educator, and author who specialized in the intersection of art, technology, and creativity. He was a Professor Emeritus of Art at the University of Calgary and was highly active in the late 1960s and 1970s. Roukes was a pioneer in the artistic use of industrial materials, particularly plastics, and authored seminal texts such as "Sculpture in Plastics" (1969), "Crafts in Plastics" (1970), and "Plastics for Kinetic Art" (1974). His work often aligned with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization he cited in his 1969 Craft Horizons article as a vital technical resource for artists. Roukes was also a significant figure in art education, developing the "Art Synectics" method to stimulate creativity by connecting disparate ideas, which he detailed in books like "Art Synectics" (1982) and "Design Synectics" (1988). Later in his career, he focused on visual humor, curating exhibitions and writing the book "Artful Jesters" (2003). He was born and raised in San Jose, California, and held degrees from California State University, Fresno, and Stanford University.

results.possibleIdentity: Martin Cohen is a New York-based photographer and mechanical engineer who became a significant figure in the arts during the 1960s and 1970s. Originally an engineering student in the Bronx, he founded the company Latin Percussion (LP) in 1964, utilizing his technical background to design and manufacture innovative instruments like fiberglass congas and bongos. Beyond his engineering work, Cohen is a prolific artist and photographer known for documenting the New York Latin jazz and salsa scene, particularly the performances at the Palladium Ballroom. His photography archive, which began in the 1950s and continued through the late 1960s and 1970s, is considered an unparalleled record of that musical era. His background as an engineer who collaborated with performers to develop new musical technologies aligns with the interdisciplinary goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization founded in 1966 to promote collaborations between artists and engineers.

Martin Cohen was an art critic and scholar active in the late 1960s who is best known for his influential article "Japan's Gutai Group," published in the November-December 1968 issue of 'Art in America'. This specific issue was a seminal publication focusing on Art and Technology, featuring discussions on the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization and its international collaborations. Cohen's work provided one of the first major Western introductions to the Gutai Art Association, an avant-garde Japanese group that collaborated closely with E.A.T. during projects like the Pepsi Pavilion at the 1970 World Exposition in Osaka (Expo '70). His scholarship in the late 1960s and early 1970s often bridged the gap between Japanese contemporary art and Western audiences.

results.possibleIdentity: Glenn Stiegelman (also frequently spelled Glenn Steigelman) is an American photographer and documentarian primarily known for his extensive work documenting contemporary art and the New York avant-garde from the late 1960s through the early 21st century. He was active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s, a period coinciding with the height of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement. Stiegelman is closely associated with E.A.T. co-founder Robert Rauschenberg, serving as a primary photographer for Rauschenberg’s works, including the "Spreads" (1975–83) and other technologically and materially complex series. His photography is credited in major retrospectives for artists such as Jasper Johns, Marcel Duchamp, Richard Serra, and Al Taylor. Through his firm, Glenn Steigelman, Inc., he provided professional photography for prominent institutions including the Leo Castelli Gallery, the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), and the Robert Rauschenberg Foundation. His work has been featured in seminal publications like Andy Warhol's Interview magazine (dating back to at least 1969) and art history textbooks such as 'Art Since 1940'. In more recent years, he has been associated with design projects, such as the renovation of a historical shack in Joshua Tree with artist Steve Halterman.
